summary:
The 8.2-kiloyear event (also written the 8.2 ka event) was a rapid and severe climate cooling episode that occurred approximately 6200 to 6050 BCE, lasting roughly 150 years and representing the most significant abrupt climate change event of the Holocene epoch. Global temperatures dropped by an estimated 1 to 3 degrees Celsius on average, with the most severe effects felt across the North Atlantic region, northwestern Europe, and parts of the Near East and Africa. The event was driven by a massive outburst of cold meltwater from proglacial lakes — primarily Lake Agassiz and Lake Ojibway in North America — into the North Atlantic, disrupting the ocean thermohaline circulation (the 'ocean conveyor belt') that regulates much of the Northern Hemisphere's climate.

The consequences of the 8.2 ka event for human populations were significant. In the Near East, proxy evidence from pollen records, sediment cores, and other paleoclimate archives indicates a period of reduced rainfall and agricultural stress coinciding with the event. Some archaeologists have proposed that the 8.2 ka event contributed to population movements, settlement abandonment, and cultural disruptions in the Neolithic Near East, including possibly contributing to the spread of farming communities westward and northward — as drought-stressed populations in the core agricultural zone sought new territories or as disrupted communities reorganized.

In Europe, the 8.2 ka event caused a period of cold and dry conditions that may have temporarily slowed the northward expansion of Neolithic farming from the Balkans. The event is also possibly connected to increased flooding in coastal and lowland areas as glacial meltwater raised sea levels, affecting coastal communities including those in the North Sea lowlands. The [[event:1479|Storegga Slide Tsunami]] off Norway occurred within a similar timeframe and may have been related to ice sheet instability associated with the same broader period of glacial change.

The 8.2 ka event serves as an important analog for studying how human societies respond to rapid climate change, a question of great contemporary relevance. Archaeological and palaeoclimatic research continues to refine understanding of its magnitude, geographic extent, and human consequences.
